annotations.label: Deterministic finite automaton (DFA)

anchor.snippet: is called a deterministic finite automaton

annotations.note: The `DFA` structure (step, start, accept over types α, σ) is the standard DFA, generalized to allow infinite state types.

annotations.label: Nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA)

anchor.snippet: does not need to obey these restrictions

annotations.note: The `NFA` structure has a set-valued transition function and a set of start states, matching the NFA definition (generalized to infinite states).

annotations.label: Every DFA is an NFA

anchor.snippet: every DFA is also an NFA

annotations.note: `DFA.toNFA` converts a DFA to an NFA and `DFA.toNFA_correct` proves the language is preserved.

annotations.label: Subset construction yields equivalent DFA

anchor.snippet: each NFA can be translated to an equivalent DFA

annotations.note: `NFA.toDFA` is the subset construction and `NFA.toDFA_correct` proves `M.toDFA.accepts = M.accepts`.

annotations.label: NFAs recognize only regular languages

anchor.snippet: NFAs only recognize regular languages

annotations.note: Follows from `NFA.toDFA_correct` together with the `Language.IsRegular` definition, but no single lemma states `M.accepts.IsRegular` directly.

annotations.label: NFA as a 5-tuple

anchor.snippet: An NFA is represented formally by a 5- tuple

annotations.note: The `NFA` structure encodes the (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) tuple with Q, Σ as type parameters and a set of start states.

annotations.label: Recognized language of an NFA

anchor.snippet: its recognized language is denoted by

annotations.note: `NFA.accepts` is the language of words for which some accept state is reachable, matching L(M).

annotations.label: Acceptance via state sequence

anchor.snippet: there are several equivalent formal definitions of a string

annotations.note: `NFA.accepts_iff_exists_path` characterizes acceptance via the existence of a `NFA.Path` (a sequence of states r₀…rₙ).

annotations.label: Acceptance via recursive reachable-set definition

anchor.snippet: is defined recursively by

annotations.note: `NFA.evalFrom` (a `List.foldl` of `stepSet`) is exactly the recursively-extended reachable-set transition function δ*.

annotations.label: Multiple initial states reducible to single initial state

anchor.snippet: There is an easy construction that translates an NFA with multiple initial states to an NFA with a single initial state

annotations.note: Mathlib's `NFA` already permits a set of start states, but the reduction construction to a single start state is not formalized.

annotations.label: NFA M accepting strings ending in 1

anchor.snippet: determines if the input ends with a 1

annotations.note: This specific concrete example automaton is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: DFA is a special kind of NFA

anchor.snippet: can be seen as a special kind of NFA

annotations.note: `DFA.toNFA` embeds a DFA into an NFA with singleton transition sets, with `DFA.toNFA_correct` confirming equivalence.

annotations.label: Every NFA has an equivalent DFA (powerset construction)

anchor.snippet: for each NFA, there is a DFA such that it recognizes the same formal language

annotations.note: `NFA.toDFA` is the powerset/subset construction and `NFA.toDFA_correct` proves it recognizes the same language.

annotations.label: Exponential state blowup of powerset construction

anchor.snippet: the resulting DFA may have up to 2 n states

annotations.note: `NFA.toDFA` uses `Set σ` as states but no cardinality bound (2^n) on the resulting DFA is stated.

annotations.label: NFA with ε-moves (NFA-ε)

anchor.snippet: is a further generalization to NFA

annotations.note: `εNFA` has a transition function on `Option α`, where `none` encodes ε-moves.

annotations.label: NFA-ε as a 5-tuple

anchor.snippet: An NFA-ε is represented formally by a 5- tuple

annotations.note: The `εNFA` structure encodes the (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) tuple with ε-transitions via `Option`, generalized to a set of start states.

annotations.label: ε-closure of a state

anchor.section: ε-closure of a state or set of states
anchor.snippet: denote the set of states that are reachable from

annotations.note: `εNFA.εClosure` of a singleton `{s}` gives the ε-closure of a single state; Mathlib defines it for any set.

annotations.label: ε-closure of a set of states

anchor.section: ε-closure of a state or set of states
anchor.snippet: of states of an NFA is defined as the set of states reachable from any state in

annotations.note: `εNFA.εClosure S` is the inductively-defined set reachable from any state of `S` by ε-transitions.

annotations.label: Extended transition function of NFA-ε

anchor.snippet: of an NFA-ε can be extended to strings

annotations.note: `εNFA.evalFrom`/`εNFA.eval` extend the ε-step function to strings via folding `stepSet` after taking ε-closures.

annotations.label: Acceptance by an NFA-ε

anchor.snippet: The automaton is said to accept a string

annotations.note: `εNFA.accepts` is the language of words whose evaluation reaches an accept state.

annotations.label: NFA-ε for even number of 0s or 1s

anchor.snippet: that determines if the input contains an even number of 0s or an even number of 1s

annotations.note: This specific concrete ε-NFA example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every NFA-ε has an equivalent NFA

anchor.snippet: for every NFA-ε, there exists an equivalent NFA

annotations.note: `εNFA.toNFA` builds an equivalent ε-free NFA and `εNFA.toNFA_correct` proves the languages agree.

annotations.label: Equality of extended transition functions by induction

anchor.snippet: by induction on the length of

annotations.note: `εNFA.toNFA_evalFrom_match` establishes the evalFrom (extended transition) functions of the ε-NFA and its constructed NFA coincide.

annotations.label: NFA-ε is equivalent to DFA

anchor.snippet: NFA-ε is also equivalent to DFA

annotations.note: Obtainable by composing `εNFA.toNFA` with `NFA.toDFA`, but no single decl states ε-NFA/DFA equivalence directly.

annotations.label: NFA-recognized languages closed under operations

anchor.snippet: The set of languages recognized by NFAs is closed under the following operations

annotations.note: Closure under union, intersection, complement, and reversal is formalized for `Language.IsRegular`, but concatenation and Kleene star closure are not.

annotations.label: Closure under union

anchor.snippet: an NFA A u can be constructed that accepts the language

annotations.note: `Language.IsRegular.add` proves regular (NFA-recognizable) languages are closed under union.

annotations.label: Closure under intersection

anchor.snippet: an NFA A i can be constructed that accepts

annotations.note: `Language.IsRegular.inf` proves regular languages are closed under intersection (via the product DFA).

annotations.label: Closure under concatenation

anchor.snippet: Concatenation

annotations.note: No lemma stating regular/NFA-recognizable languages are closed under concatenation (`*`) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Closure under negation

anchor.snippet: an NFA A n can be constructed that accepts

annotations.note: `Language.IsRegular.compl` proves regular languages are closed under complement.

annotations.label: Closure under Kleene closure

anchor.snippet: Kleene closure

annotations.note: No lemma stating regular/NFA-recognizable languages are closed under Kleene star exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Language accepted by an NFA is regular

anchor.snippet: This language is a regular language

annotations.note: Follows from `NFA.toDFA_correct` plus `Language.IsRegular`, but no direct `NFA.accepts.IsRegular` lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Every NFA has an equivalent DFA

anchor.snippet: For every NFA a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) can be found that accepts the same language

annotations.note: `NFA.toDFA_correct` proves the constructed DFA accepts the same language as the NFA.

annotations.label: Time and space bound for NFA simulation

anchor.snippet: A string of length n can be processed in time

annotations.note: Mathlib has no computational-complexity (time/space) analysis of NFA simulation.

annotations.label: Emptiness problem solvable in linear time

anchor.snippet: One can solve in linear time the emptiness problem for NFA

annotations.note: The NFA emptiness problem and its complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universality of NFA is PSPACE-complete

anchor.snippet: It is PSPACE -complete to test, given an NFA, whether it is universal

annotations.note: Mathlib has no PSPACE-completeness or complexity-class framework for NFA universality.

annotations.label: Inclusion problem is PSPACE-complete

anchor.snippet: the same is true of the inclusion problem

annotations.note: The NFA language-inclusion problem and its PSPACE-completeness are not formalized.

annotations.label: Counting accepted words is #P-hard

anchor.snippet: the counting problem of determining how many words of length n are accepted by A is intractable

annotations.note: Counting accepted words and #P-hardness are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NFAs and DFAs are equivalent

anchor.snippet: NFAs and DFAs are equivalent in that if a language is recognized by an NFA

annotations.note: `NFA.toDFA_correct` and `DFA.toNFA_correct` together establish that NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same languages.
